pi_items.source: Confirmed in setup_task.sh line 10 and export_result.sh line 7, plus hardcoded in export analysis
Confirmed in setup_task.sh line 11 and export_result.sh line 8, plus used in export analysis

Verifier.py lines 31 and 75 claim this threshold differentiates solid skull (~230k triangles) from hollow shell (~430k triangles). DICOM dataset analysis: 108 slices × 512 × 512 pixels = 28.3M voxels. Marching cubes generates 1-4 triangles per cube. For surface structures representing ~1-5% of volume, estimates range 140k-560k triangles. The threshold of 300k is reasonable but exact numbers (230k, 430k) could not be independently verified without executing InVesalius.

Task description explicitly states workflow: (1) Create primary bone mask, (2) Create core mask by eroding, (3) Create hollow shell by subtracting. Export_result.sh line 74 counts masks in tar file. Verified: at least 3 masks expected (bone, core, shell).

data_provenance.data_type: Real CT imaging data from human cranium

privileged_info_summary: The task uses real CT imaging data (DICOM 0051) from the official InVesalius GitHub repository, containing 108 slices of 512×512 pixel cranial CT images. The task expects 3 masks (original bone, eroded core, and hollow shell via boolean subtraction) and a hollow shell STL with >300,000 triangles. The output paths are /home/ga/Documents/hollow_skull.stl and /home/ga/Documents/hollow_project.inv3. The triangle count threshold of 300,000 is designed to differentiate solid skull geometry (~230,000 triangles) from hollow shell geometry (~430,000 triangles), leveraging the fact that a shell has both inner and outer surfaces.
